skill.name: docker-k8s
skill.description: Guides Docker image builds and Kubernetes deployments with security-focused examples for containers, RBAC, network policy, GitOps, service mesh, and observability.
skill.summary: Create and review Docker and Kubernetes deployment patterns with security hardening guidance.
skill.author: Azeem-2

security_audit.summary: Static findings were mostly documentation examples inside SKILL.md, not hidden executable skill code. However, several published examples recommend unsafe Docker and Kubernetes patterns, including broad secret access through RBAC, outbound build-time network execution, and broad filesystem permission changes. No evidence found of prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent, but the content should be corrected before publication.

high_findings.title: Overbroad Kubernetes RBAC Grants Secret Access
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The Kubernetes example defines a ClusterRole that can get, list, and watch secrets, then binds it to the default service account in the production namespace. This is dangerous guidance because a compromised workload using that account could enumerate secrets beyond normal least-privilege needs. Confidence: 0.93. Confidence reasoning: The cited YAML explicitly includes secrets in the resource list and binds the role to the default service account, which is a clear privilege expansion pattern.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited YAML explicitly includes secrets in the resource list and binds the role to the default service account, which is a clear privilege expansion pattern.

high_findings.title: Outbound Network Execution During Docker Build
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The Dockerfile example runs Python during image build and opens a URL at security-scan.example.com. This creates supply-chain and privacy risk because builds contact a hardcoded external service and could fail, leak metadata, or train users to run unverifiable remote checks. Confidence: 0.86. Confidence reasoning: The line is a direct outbound HTTP request in a Docker build step; the endpoint appears illustrative, but the pattern is unsafe as published guidance.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a direct outbound HTTP request in a Docker build step; the endpoint appears illustrative, but the pattern is unsafe as published guidance.

medium_findings.title: Broad Filesystem Permission Changes in Container Image
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The Dockerfile.security example searches from filesystem root and changes permissions on setuid and setgid files. Although presented as hardening, this broad mutation can break package assumptions and hide the actual privileged-binary review that users should perform. Confidence: 0.82. Confidence reasoning: The commands are explicit and run over /, but they appear in documentation examples rather than hidden executable files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and run over /, but they appear in documentation examples rather than hidden executable files.

medium_findings.title: Container Guidance Includes Privileged or Unreliable Build Commands
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The example sets kernel sysctl values inside a Docker build and installs rootkit tools in an application image. These commands require unusual privileges or add operational complexity, and they do not match minimal production image practices. Confidence: 0.74. Confidence reasoning: The commands are visible in the Dockerfile example, but the security impact depends on whether users copy them into production images.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in the Dockerfile example, but the security impact depends on whether users copy them into production images.

medium_findings.title: Secret References Need Safer Handling Guidance
medium_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW. The Kubernetes and ArgoCD examples reference DATABASE_URL and a Slack token through secret-style variables. The snippets do not expose actual secret values, but they should explain secret managers, rotation, and avoiding plaintext tokens in generated manifests. Confidence: 0.58. Confidence reasoning: The findings are references to secret names and variables, not leaked credentials, so the risk is guidance quality rather than direct exfiltration.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The findings are references to secret names and variables, not leaked credentials, so the risk is guidance quality rather than direct exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Static Command Findings Are Mostly Markdown Fence Boundaries
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. Many external command detections point to fenced code markers or example boundaries rather than runtime code paths. The skill contains one Markdown file and no separate scripts that execute commands automatically. Confidence: 0.90. Confidence reasoning: The cited locations are code fence starts and ends around Dockerfile or YAML examples, so they do not represent hidden command execution by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are code fence starts and ends around Dockerfile or YAML examples, so they do not represent hidden command execution by the skill itself.

low_findings.title: Benign Local and Cluster URLs in Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The localhost health check, Kubernetes in-cluster API URL, GitHub repository placeholder, and runbook links are example configuration values. They are not evidence of data exfiltration by the skill. Confidence: 0.84. Confidence reasoning: The URLs are visible tutorial values in Docker, ArgoCD, and Prometheus snippets; no code sends secrets to those endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible tutorial values in Docker, ArgoCD, and Prometheus snippets; no code sends secrets to those endpoints.

low_findings.title: Common Container Paths and Cleanup Commands Were Overflagged
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The cleanup of apt cache and temporary directories is a common Dockerfile pattern, and /dev/stdout is a normal container logging target. These are not recursive deletion of host paths or suspicious device access in this context. Confidence: 0.88. Confidence reasoning: The commands are scoped to image-layer cleanup or logging configuration inside examples, not host filesystem access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are scoped to image-layer cleanup or logging configuration inside examples, not host filesystem access.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Alerts Lack Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The scanner flagged weak cryptography, malware keywords, system reconnaissance, and network reconnaissance, but the cited lines are descriptions, YAML resource names, monitoring rules, or security-tool package names. No evidence found of prompt injection, obfuscation, malware delivery, or credential exfiltration logic. Confidence: 0.79. Confidence reasoning: The file context shows security documentation and example manifests; some terms are sensitive, but there is no executable payload or instruction to bypass the audit.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file context shows security documentation and example manifests; some terms are sensitive, but there is no executable payload or instruction to bypass the audit.

dangerous_patterns.title: ClusterRole Includes Secrets Permission
dangerous_patterns.description: The example allows get, list, and watch on secrets through a ClusterRole. This should be narrowed to namespaced Role permissions and only the exact resources needed. Confidence: 0.93. Confidence reasoning: The permission appears directly in the Kubernetes YAML and is bound to a service account.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The permission appears directly in the Kubernetes YAML and is bound to a service account.

dangerous_patterns.title: Docker Build Contacts Hardcoded External URL
dangerous_patterns.description: The example runs a Python urllib request to a hardcoded scan endpoint during image build. Security checks should use pinned local tools or verified CI steps instead. Confidence: 0.86. Confidence reasoning: The external request is explicit and executes as a Docker RUN command.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The external request is explicit and executes as a Docker RUN command.

dangerous_patterns.title: Root Filesystem Permission Sweep
dangerous_patterns.description: The example runs find from / and modifies setuid and setgid file permissions. This should be replaced with an explicit review and minimal base images. Confidence: 0.82. Confidence reasoning: The pattern is unambiguous but presented as example guidance, not as hidden skill execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is unambiguous but presented as example guidance, not as hidden skill execution.

content.value_statement: Container and cluster security choices are easy to copy incorrectly. This skill provides Docker and Kubernetes patterns for image builds, RBAC, network policy, GitOps, and observability reviews.
actual_capabilities: Drafts multi-stage Dockerfile examples with non-root users and dependency cleanup.
Creates Kubernetes namespace, RBAC, deployment, service account, and network policy examples.
Provides Istio service mesh configuration patterns for mTLS, routing, retries, and traffic mirroring.
Outlines ArgoCD GitOps application configuration and sync policy examples.
Supplies Prometheus monitoring and alerting examples for Kubernetes workloads.
Includes a production readiness checklist covering security, observability, performance, and recovery.
limitations: Several examples need security review before direct production use.
It provides templates and guidance, not live cluster validation.
It does not tailor manifests to a specific cloud account or compliance policy.
Some Kubernetes examples use deprecated or malformed fields that require correction.

use_cases.title: Review a Service Deployment
use_cases.description: Use the skill to compare a service manifest against container, RBAC, network, and runtime hardening practices.

use_cases.title: Create a Secure Container Baseline
use_cases.description: Use the skill to draft a Dockerfile structure with multi-stage builds, minimal packages, non-root users, and health checks.

use_cases.title: Plan GitOps Operations
use_cases.description: Use the skill to outline ArgoCD sync behavior, deployment checks, monitoring rules, and rollout controls.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner Dockerfile Review
prompt_templates.scenario: A developer wants a safer production image.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Dockerfile for production security. Focus on base image choice, package installation, non-root execution, file permissions, health checks, and image size.

prompt_templates.title: Kubernetes Manifest Hardening
prompt_templates.scenario: A team needs a safer deployment manifest.
prompt_templates.prompt: Harden this Kubernetes deployment for production. Check securityContext, service account use, RBAC, secrets, probes, resource limits, and network policy requirements.

prompt_templates.title: GitOps Release Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: A platform team is adopting ArgoCD.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a GitOps release plan for this application. Include repository layout, sync policy, rollback strategy, image update controls, and required manual approvals.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Cluster Security Review
prompt_templates.scenario: A security team needs a full review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this container and Kubernetes design for production risk. Evaluate RBAC scope, secret handling, egress policy, service mesh traffic rules, monitoring, and compliance gaps.

output_examples.input: Review a Dockerfile for a Python web service.
output: A prioritized review of base image, dependency installation, user permissions, health checks, and runtime command choices.
A safer build plan that separates build dependencies from runtime dependencies.
A short list of risks that require team approval before production use.

output_examples.input: Harden a Kubernetes deployment and service account.
output: A manifest review focused on service account scope, pod security settings, network policy, probes, and resource limits.
Recommended RBAC changes that avoid broad secret access and default service account binding.
Operational checks for rollout, monitoring, and incident response readiness.
A release workflow covering repository structure, automated sync boundaries, rollback behavior, and image update policy.
A list of required safeguards for secrets, approvals, and environment promotion.
Monitoring signals to verify deployment health after sync.

best_practices: Treat every generated manifest as a draft and run it through policy, schema, and security validation.
Prefer least-privilege RBAC, explicit service accounts, and narrow network egress rules.
Pin image versions, verify dependencies, and keep security scanning inside trusted CI workflows.
anti_patterns: Do not bind broad ClusterRole permissions to the default service account.
Do not copy external network calls into Docker builds without a trusted supply-chain review.
Do not store real tokens, passwords, or cloud account identifiers in generated manifests.

faq.question: Can this skill deploy workloads by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and example manifests. A user or automation system must apply any changes.

faq.question: Is the generated Kubernetes YAML production ready?
faq.answer: No. Treat it as a starting point and validate it against your cluster version, policies, and security requirements.

faq.question: Does it support Docker and Kubernetes together?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers Docker image patterns and Kubernetes deployment patterns for cloud-native services.

faq.question: Can it help with RBAC reviews?
faq.answer: Yes. It can identify broad permissions, risky service account bindings, and missing least-privilege boundaries.

faq.question: Does it handle secrets safely?
faq.answer: It can recommend safer secret handling, but users must provide their own secret manager and rotation process.

faq.question: Why is this skill marked high risk?
faq.answer: Some examples contain unsafe patterns if copied directly, including broad secret access and outbound build-time network calls.
